summary: The post examines a whitepaper about the White House Police Data Initiative, which encourages police departments to share their data publicly. O'Neil argues that the fundamental problem isn't transparency but data collection itself—police departments simply don't gather information that might reflect poorly on them. She points out that even basic data like fatal police shootings lacks a reliable national registry, and information about public trust in police or jail conditions remains systematically uncollected. The piece concludes with skepticism about reform, noting that facilities like Rikers can't even be trusted to accurately count inmate fights.
key_points: Police departments only collect data that makes them look efficient or successful, creating systematic blind spots
The 'open data' initiative is fundamentally flawed because you cannot open data that was never collected
Voluntary data collection efforts have failed, leaving journalists like The Guardian to track police shootings better than the FBI
Critical information about public trust in police and conditions for arrested individuals remains uncollected
Even when data exists, institutions like Rikers have been caught manipulating basic statistics
money_quotes: You can't open up data that you don't collect.
When we ask police departments to 'open up' their data, we are assuming they collect the data we want. But they only collect the data that makes them look efficient or successful.
We don't have a high-quality national register of fatal police shootings, and the Guardian has a better one.
They cannot even be trusted to count inmate fights.
key_insight: Transparency initiatives fail when they assume institutions collect inconvenient data—the real problem is that powerful actors systematically avoid measuring what might hold them accountable.
